content.text: Rather than crafting prompts by hand, meta-prompting delegates that job to the model itself: one LLM call produces the instructions that a second LLM call executes. This level of indirection transforms prompting from artisanal guesswork into a systematic, automatable engineering practice — and is foundational to modern automatic prompt optimization pipelines.
Meta-prompting is a technique in which a language model is instructed to generate, critique, or refine a prompt that will be used to solve a downstream task. The term operates at two levels: the
(where a prompt is constructed or improved) and the

(where the constructed prompt is executed to produce a result). In the simplest form, a meta-prompt says something like: "Write a system prompt that will make an LLM perform X as accurately as possible." A more sophisticated version includes an evaluation loop: the generated prompt is tested on sample inputs, scored, and the score is fed back to the meta-level LLM to produce a better prompt.

The concept spans several related but distinct patterns: single-pass meta-prompting (generate once), iterative meta-prompting (generate-evaluate-refine loop), self-play (same model at both levels), cross-model meta-prompting (different models at each level), and population-based search (sample many candidate prompts and select the best). All share the same core property: prompt content is treated as an optimizable variable, not as a fixed engineering artifact.

Manual prompt engineering is labor-intensive, brittle, and difficult to transfer across tasks. A prompt carefully tuned for one use case often degrades when the input distribution shifts, the underlying model is updated, or the task requirements evolve. Human engineers must re-invest significant effort every time, and the quality ceiling is limited by the engineer's own intuition about what the model responds to.

Meta-prompting addresses these issues by making the model itself the primary author of prompts. Models have been trained on vastly more examples of effective prompts than any individual human has written. They can generate dozens of candidate phrasings, critique their own outputs, and systematically explore variations that a human would not think to try. When paired with an objective evaluation function — even an LLM-as-judge — this creates a search process over prompt space that is both scalable and task-adaptive.

In production agentic systems, meta-prompting enables agents to adapt their own instructions based on feedback, bootstrap specialized sub-agent prompts from high-level task descriptions, and continuously improve performance without requiring engineering intervention.
: Engineers describe the task goal at a high level; the model generates the detailed prompt, eliminating hours of trial-and-error tuning.
: The same meta-prompting harness can generate optimized prompts for hundreds of distinct sub-tasks in a pipeline without per-task manual engineering.
: The model is aware of its own strengths and failure modes and can incorporate that awareness into the prompts it writes.
: When model versions or task distributions change, the meta-prompting loop can re-run to regenerate prompts appropriate for the new context without human re-intervention.
: Unlike gradient-based soft-prompt tuning, meta-prompting produces human-readable natural-language prompts, making them inspectable, auditable, and easy to manually override.
: Works with any API-accessible model, including closed-weight commercial APIs, without needing backpropagation or fine-tuning infrastructure.
: The meta-level LLM call adds latency and cost on top of the task-level call. In iterative loops, cost scales with the number of optimization rounds.
: The quality of optimization is bounded by the quality of the evaluation signal. Noisy or misaligned evaluators cause the loop to optimize for the wrong objective.
: Without careful temperature control and sampling strategy, iterative loops can oscillate between good and bad prompts rather than converging.
: If the evaluation examples are not representative of production inputs, the optimized prompt may perform poorly on real traffic even though it scores well in the loop.
: A prompt optimized against one model checkpoint may degrade when the model is updated, requiring re-optimization.
: Models tend to generate long, instruction-heavy prompts. Without constraints, meta-prompting can produce prompts that consume disproportionate context budget.
At its core, meta-prompting treats a prompt
as a free parameter in a quality function
is a set of evaluation examples. The goal is to find a prompt that maximizes this quality:
Because the prompt space is a discrete, high-dimensional space of natural language strings, gradient descent cannot be applied directly. Meta-prompting instead performs

over this space, using the LLM itself as the proposal distribution. The meta-level LLM generates candidate prompts conditioned on the task description and, in iterative variants, on previous prompts along with their scores. This is analogous to Bayesian optimization where the LLM acts as a surrogate-free acquisition function.
The canonical iterative variant proceeds as follows. In round
, the meta-LLM is given the task description, the current best prompt
. The revised prompt is then evaluated on
, the new prompt is accepted; otherwise the previous best is retained. The loop runs for a fixed number of rounds or until convergence (no improvement over
A key refinement used by algorithms like ProTeGi (Prompt Optimization with Textual Gradients) and DSPy's MIPRO optimizer is replacing a numeric score with a

. Rather than passing "score: 0.62" to the meta-LLM, the evaluator produces: "The prompt lacks explicit output format instructions, causing the model to return verbose prose when JSON is expected. Add a JSON schema constraint." This textual gradient tells the meta-LLM exactly what aspect of the prompt to change, dramatically reducing the search space and accelerating convergence.

The diagram below shows the canonical meta-prompting pipeline. A user goal flows into the meta-prompt generator, which uses a language model to produce an optimized prompt. That prompt is passed to the task LLM, whose output is scored by the evaluator. The evaluation feedback (numeric score or textual critique) loops back to the meta-prompt generator to drive refinement.

attrs.alt: Meta-Prompting flow: user goal through meta-prompt generator, optimized prompt to task LLM, evaluation feedback loop

content.text: Meta-prompting is most valuable when the prompt-quality gap is measurable and the cost of suboptimal prompts is high. The following scenarios are strong candidates:
where even a few percentage points of accuracy improvement translates to meaningful downstream value (e.g., medical coding, contract clause extraction).
: When building a multi-step agentic pipeline and you need per-step system prompts quickly, meta-prompting provides a solid first draft for each step without manual authoring.
: When prompts must be tailored to domain vocabulary, tone, or format requirements that the generic model does not follow by default.
: After a model update, running a meta-prompting re-optimization pass guards against silent prompt regressions.

Avoid meta-prompting when latency is severely constrained and offline optimization is impractical, when evaluation examples are too few to distinguish good prompts from bad ones (fewer than ~20 labeled examples), or when the task is so open-ended that quality cannot be quantified (e.g., creative writing with purely subjective quality).
A minimal meta-prompting implementation requires three components: a meta-prompt template, an evaluation function, and a loop controller. The following steps describe the setup:
: A clear natural-language statement of what the task-level LLM should do, including expected input and output format.
: Collect 20-100 input/expected-output pairs that represent the real input distribution. These drive the quality signal.
: Exact match, F1, BLEU, LLM-as-judge score, or a custom domain metric. The choice determines what the optimizer converges on.

: A prompt that instructs the meta-LLM to produce a system prompt. Include the task description, any constraints (token budget, output format), and — in later rounds — the previous prompt and its critique.
: For each round, call the meta-LLM to generate a candidate prompt, evaluate it on the eval set, and collect the score or critique. Retain the best candidate.
: Run the final prompt on a held-out test set to confirm generalization before deploying to production.
The simplest form: ask an LLM to write a prompt for a specific task, then execute that prompt on real inputs.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Step 1: Use the meta-LLM to generate a task prompt
def generate_task_prompt(task_description: str) -> str:
    meta_prompt = f"""You are a prompt engineering expert.

Write a system prompt for an AI assistant that will: {task_description}

Requirements:
- Be specific about the desired output format
- Include guidance on tone and depth
- Handle edge cases the model might misinterpret
- Keep the prompt under 300 words

Return ONLY the system prompt text, with no explanation."""

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=500,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": meta_prompt}]
    )
    return response.content[0].text


# Step 2: Execute the generated prompt on a real task
def execute_with_prompt(system_prompt: str, user_input: str) -> str:
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        system=system_prompt,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_input}]
    )
    return response.content[0].text


if __name__ == "__main__":
    task = (
        "Extract all named entities (people, organizations, locations) "
        "from user-provided text and return them as a JSON object with "
        "keys 'people', 'organizations', 'locations', each containing a list of strings."
    )

    print("Generating optimized prompt...")
    generated_prompt = generate_task_prompt(task)
    print(f"Generated prompt:\n{generated_prompt}\n")

    test_input = (
        "Elon Musk met with Angela Merkel in Berlin last Tuesday to discuss "
        "a new partnership between Tesla and the European Commission."
    )
    result = execute_with_prompt(generated_prompt, test_input)
    print(f"Task output:\n{result}")

This example implements a full generate-evaluate-critique-refine loop. The evaluator uses exact-match accuracy on a labeled dataset and converts the error analysis into a textual critique for the next round.

import json
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

@dataclass
class EvalExample:
    input_text: str
    expected_output: str


EVAL_SET = [
    EvalExample("The sky is blue.", "positive"),
    EvalExample("I hate waiting in long lines.", "negative"),
    EvalExample("The food was okay, nothing special.", "neutral"),
    EvalExample("Best concert I've ever attended!", "positive"),
    EvalExample("Terrible customer service experience.", "negative"),
    EvalExample("It arrived on time.", "neutral"),
    EvalExample("Absolutely love this product.", "positive"),
    EvalExample("Broken on arrival, very disappointed.", "negative"),
]


def evaluate_prompt(prompt: str) -> tuple[float, list[dict]]:
    """Run the prompt on eval set, return (accuracy, error_cases)."""
    correct = 0
    errors = []
    for ex in EVAL_SET:
        resp = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-haiku-4-5",
            max_tokens=10,
            system=prompt,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": ex.input_text}]
        )
        pred = resp.content[0].text.strip().lower()
        if ex.expected_output in pred:
            correct += 1
        else:
            errors.append({"input": ex.input_text, "expected": ex.expected_output, "got": pred})
    return correct / len(EVAL_SET), errors


def generate_critique(task: str, prompt: str, score: float, errors: list[dict]) -> str:
    """Ask meta-LLM to generate a textual critique given the failure cases."""
    errors_str = json.dumps(errors, indent=2)
    critique_request = f"""
Task: {task}
Current system prompt:
---
{prompt}
---
Accuracy: {score:.0%}
Failed cases:
{errors_str}

Identify the specific weaknesses in the current prompt that caused these failures.
Return a short, actionable critique (max 150 words)."""
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=300,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": critique_request}]
    )
    return resp.content[0].text


def refine_prompt(task: str, current_prompt: str, critique: str) -> str:
    """Ask meta-LLM to produce an improved prompt given the critique."""
    refine_request = f"""
Task: {task}
Current system prompt:
---
{current_prompt}
---
Critique: {critique}

Write an improved system prompt that addresses the critique.
Return ONLY the new system prompt text."""
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=600,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": refine_request}]
    )
    return resp.content[0].text


def meta_prompting_loop(
    task: str,
    initial_prompt: str,
    max_rounds: int = 5,
    target_score: float = 0.95,
) -> tuple[str, float]:
    """Iterative meta-prompting: refine until target score or max rounds."""
    best_prompt = initial_prompt
    best_score, errors = evaluate_prompt(initial_prompt)
    print(f"Round 0: score={best_score:.0%}")

    for round_num in range(1, max_rounds + 1):
        if best_score >= target_score:
            break
        critique = generate_critique(task, best_prompt, best_score, errors)
        candidate = refine_prompt(task, best_prompt, critique)
        score, errors = evaluate_prompt(candidate)
        print(f"Round {round_num}: score={score:.0%}")
        if score > best_score:
            best_score, best_prompt = score, candidate

    return best_prompt, best_score


if __name__ == "__main__":
    TASK = "Classify the sentiment of the input text as 'positive', 'negative', or 'neutral'. Output only the single label word."
    INITIAL = "Classify the sentiment of the following text."
    final_prompt, final_score = meta_prompting_loop(TASK, INITIAL)
    print(f"\nFinal score: {final_score:.0%}")
    print(f"Final prompt:\n{final_prompt}")

A fintech company uses an LLM pipeline to classify customer support tickets into routing categories (billing, fraud, technical, account). With 50,000 tickets per day, even a 2% misrouting rate translates to 1,000 tickets handled by the wrong team. The initial hand-authored prompt achieved 89% accuracy on a test set of 500 labeled tickets.

The team ran a meta-prompting loop: 80 labeled examples formed the eval set, with a held-out 420 for final testing. The meta-LLM was given the current prompt, the accuracy, and a list of the 10 most common error patterns (e.g., "account closure requests mislabeled as billing"). Over 6 rounds, the accuracy improved from 89% to 96%. The loop was also integrated into CI: any model version bump automatically re-runs the optimization, preventing silent regressions.

# Production pattern: versioned prompt store with meta-prompting
import hashlib
import json
from pathlib import Path

PROMPT_STORE = Path("prompts/")
PROMPT_STORE.mkdir(exist_ok=True)


def load_or_optimize_prompt(
    task_id: str,
    task_description: str,
    eval_fn,          # callable(prompt) -> float
    force_reoptimize: bool = False,
) -> str:
    """
    Load a cached optimized prompt, or run meta-prompting to create one.
    Keys the cache on (task_id, model_id) to detect model-version changes.
    """
    import anthropic
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    model_id = "claude-haiku-4-5"
    version_key = hashlib.md5(
        f"{task_id}:{model_id}".encode()
    ).hexdigest()[:8]
    cache_path = PROMPT_STORE / f"{task_id}_{version_key}.json"

    if cache_path.exists() and not force_reoptimize:
        data = json.loads(cache_path.read_text())
        print(f"Loaded cached prompt for {task_id} (score={data['score']:.0%})")
        return data["prompt"]

    seed_prompt = _generate_seed_prompt(client, task_description)
    best_prompt, best_score = _optimize_loop(seed_prompt, eval_fn, rounds=5)

    cache_path.write_text(json.dumps({"prompt": best_prompt, "score": best_score}))
    print(f"Optimized and cached prompt for {task_id} (score={best_score:.0%})")
    return best_prompt


def _generate_seed_prompt(client, task_description: str) -> str:
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=400,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content":
            f"Write a concise system prompt for an LLM that will: {task_description}\n"
            "Return only the prompt text."}]
    )
    return resp.content[0].text


def _optimize_loop(initial: str, eval_fn, rounds: int) -> tuple[str, float]:
    import anthropic
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    best, best_score = initial, eval_fn(initial)
    for _ in range(rounds):
        if best_score >= 0.97:
            break
        critique_resp = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-opus-4-5",
            max_tokens=300,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content":
                f"Current prompt: {best}\nScore: {best_score:.0%}\n"
                "List specific improvements needed (max 100 words)."}]
        )
        refine_resp = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-opus-4-5",
            max_tokens=500,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content":
                f"Improve this prompt based on: {critique_resp.content[0].text}\n"
                f"Original: {best}\nReturn only the new prompt."}]
        )
        candidate = refine_resp.content[0].text
        score = eval_fn(candidate)
        if score > best_score:
            best, best_score = candidate, score
    return best, best_score

Using the same examples for both optimization and final evaluation inflates the apparent accuracy. The optimized prompt memorizes the eval set rather than generalizing. Always hold out a separate test partition that the optimizer never sees.

Running fixed rounds without tracking the best-seen score causes regressions: a later round may produce a worse prompt that gets accepted because the loop has no memory. Always maintain a best-score variable and only update the prompt when the new score strictly improves.

A meta-prompt that says only "write a better prompt" produces generic output. The meta-LLM needs the task description, the output format requirement, any hard constraints (e.g., token limit, language), and an example of a good vs. bad response to have enough signal for useful improvement.

Without explicit constraints, each refinement round tends to add more instructions, making the prompt longer. A 2,000-token system prompt on a 128k-context model is not inherently wrong, but it adds cost to every task call. Impose a word-budget constraint in the meta-prompt and monitor prompt length across rounds.
Use a stronger meta-LLM and a weaker task LLM
: The meta-LLM needs reasoning capability; the task LLM needs speed and low cost. Using a large model for meta-level work and a small model for execution is a common and effective division.
Seed the loop with a reasonable initial prompt
: Starting from an obviously bad prompt wastes early rounds on obvious fixes. Use a manually authored or meta-generated seed that already captures the task basics.
Include both success and failure examples in the critique
: Showing the meta-LLM only what went wrong is half the picture. Providing examples it got right helps it understand what to preserve in the next version.
: Store the final prompt along with its score, the model version it was optimized for, and the date. Re-run optimization when the model changes, not on every deployment.
Set a token budget in the meta-prompt
: Explicitly instruct the meta-LLM to produce a prompt under N words. This avoids runaway verbosity without requiring post-processing.
: Instead of one candidate per round, sample 3-5 variations at temperature > 0 and select the best. This increases exploration at the cost of more eval calls.
The cost of a meta-prompting run scales as:
is the number of optimization rounds,
is the cost of one meta-LLM call,
is the eval set size, and

is the cost of one task call. For a typical setup with 5 rounds, 50 eval examples, and a haiku-class task model, the total optimization cost is on the order of a few dollars — a one-time investment that pays off across millions of production inferences.

Parallelizing the eval set calls is the single largest optimization. Evaluating 50 examples sequentially at ~200ms each takes 10 seconds per round; evaluating them concurrently with async API calls takes under 2 seconds. Use
or a thread pool executor to batch eval calls within each round.

For large-scale deployments where hundreds of distinct sub-tasks each need optimized prompts, run the meta-prompting loops in parallel (one per task). The loops are independent and can be distributed across worker processes. The only shared resource is the API rate limit, which can be managed with a semaphore or token bucket.

DSPy (Declarative Self-improving Python) is the most mature open-source framework for meta-prompting. It represents LLM pipelines as Python programs with typed signatures, and includes optimizers (MIPRO, BootstrapFewShot, COPRO) that automatically tune both prompts and few-shot examples. DSPy's COPRO optimizer performs iterative meta-prompting with LLM-generated critiques, closely matching the pattern described above.

import dspy

# Configure the language model
lm = dspy.LM("anthropic/claude-haiku-4-5")
dspy.configure(lm=lm)


# Define a typed task signature — the docstring becomes the meta-prompt seed
class SentimentClassifier(dspy.Signature):
    """Classify the sentiment of the input text."""
    text: str = dspy.InputField()
    sentiment: str = dspy.OutputField(desc="One of: positive, negative, neutral")


# Wrap in a Module
classifier = dspy.Predict(SentimentClassifier)

# Compile with COPRO (Coordinate Prompt Optimization — iterative meta-prompting)
from dspy.teleprompt import COPRO

optimizer = COPRO(
    metric=lambda pred, gold: pred.sentiment.lower() == gold.sentiment.lower(),
    breadth=5,     # candidate prompts per round
    depth=3,       # refinement rounds
    verbose=True,
)

trainset = [
    dspy.Example(text="Best product ever!", sentiment="positive").with_inputs("text"),
    dspy.Example(text="Terrible experience.", sentiment="negative").with_inputs("text"),
    dspy.Example(text="It was fine.", sentiment="neutral").with_inputs("text"),
    dspy.Example(text="I love this.", sentiment="positive").with_inputs("text"),
    dspy.Example(text="Worst service ever.", sentiment="negative").with_inputs("text"),
]

optimized_classifier = optimizer.compile(classifier, trainset=trainset)

# Inspect the generated system prompt
print("Optimized signature instructions:")
print(optimized_classifier.predict.signature.instructions)

# Use the optimized classifier
result = optimized_classifier(text="This exceeded all my expectations!")
print(f"Sentiment: {result.sentiment}")
: A prompt testing CLI that includes a "suggest" command generating prompt variations via meta-prompting and running them against a test suite.
: LangSmith's evaluation loops and hub can be combined with custom meta-prompting chains, providing observability on prompt version history and score trends.
: Provides prompt versioning and experiment tracking that integrates naturally with meta-prompting runs to compare generations across rounds.
: Includes a built-in "Generate prompt" feature that performs single-pass meta-prompting from a task description, making it useful for bootstrapping new prompts manually.
Novel tasks with no eval data

When the meta-LLM generates a system prompt that includes dynamic user-supplied content (e.g., extracted task descriptions from user messages), there is a risk of indirect prompt injection: a malicious user inserts instructions into the task description that propagate into the generated system prompt, then influence every subsequent execution. Mitigate this by sanitizing inputs to the meta-level call and never including raw user-provided text verbatim in the meta-prompt.

An unconstrained meta-prompting loop can converge on a prompt that achieves high eval score but violates safety or tone guidelines — for example, an overly authoritative tone or prompts that elicit overconfident model behavior. Include a safety checker in the eval function that penalizes policy violations, and always do a human review of the final prompt before deployment.

If the eval set is sourced from production logs, it may contain adversarial examples crafted by users trying to steer model behavior. Audit the eval set for anomalies before using it as the optimization target. Prefer manually curated, representative examples over unfiltered production samples.

Store every generated prompt with its round number, score, and timestamp. If a newly optimized prompt causes a production regression, the ability to instantly roll back to the previous version is critical. Version control for prompts follows the same discipline as version control for code.

Think of meta-prompting as hiring an experienced technical writer to draft your instructions, then having a QA engineer test them, and repeating the cycle until the instructions reliably produce the desired outcome. The technical writer (meta-LLM) knows the conventions, the language the model responds to, and can incorporate critique quickly. The QA engineer (evaluator) knows whether the instructions actually work. The engineer defines the acceptance criterion and the test cases, but delegates the actual writing and rewriting to these specialized roles.

Another useful frame: meta-prompting is Bayesian optimization over the discrete space of natural-language strings. The meta-LLM serves as the acquisition function — it picks the next candidate to evaluate based on everything known so far. The textual critique is the "gradient" that guides the acquisition function toward more promising regions of prompt space.
Meta-prompting delegates prompt authorship to the LLM itself, treating the prompt as an optimizable variable rather than a fixed engineering artifact.
The core loop is: generate candidate prompt, evaluate on labeled examples, produce textual critique, refine, repeat until convergence.
Textual critiques (what to change and why) outperform numeric scores as feedback signals because they reduce the search space and guide the meta-LLM toward specific fixes.
The quality ceiling is set by the evaluation function — noisy or misaligned evaluators cause the loop to optimize for the wrong objective.
Use a stronger model for meta-level work and a faster, cheaper model for task execution; cache and version the resulting prompts to amortize optimization cost.
Unlike soft-prompt tuning or fine-tuning, meta-prompting requires no gradient access, works with any API, and produces human-readable, auditable prompts.
DSPy's COPRO and MIPRO optimizers provide production-ready implementations of meta-prompting loops with typed signatures, few-shot management, and evaluation harnesses.
Security risks include prompt injection via generated prompts, alignment drift in unconstrained loops, and evaluation set poisoning — all require explicit mitigation.
